articles.topic: internalized stress hopelessness memory decline aging
articles.headline: Bottling Up Stress Is One of the Worst Things You Can Do for Your Brain
articles.body: A Rutgers Health study tracked over 1,500 older adults and found that people who internalize stress — absorbing it rather than expressing it — showed the strongest link to memory decline over time. Feelings of hopelessness were the key driver. Community support and stress-relief activities didn't offset it. Researchers say this form of stress often goes unnoticed because it's quiet and internal. The good news: it's considered a modifiable trait, meaning it can be changed with the right support.
articles.takeaway: If you tend to bottle things up, that habit may be costing you more than mood. Finding an outlet — a therapist, a trusted friend, even a journal — isn't soft. It's brain protection.

articles.topic: CAG-170 gut bacteria healthy microbiome discovery
articles.headline: Scientists Found a Mystery Gut Bacteria That Keeps Showing Up in Healthy People
articles.body: Researchers at the University of Cambridge analyzed gut microbiome samples from more than 11,000 people across 39 countries and found one group of bacteria — called CAG-170 — consistently appeared in healthy people and was far less common in those with chronic diseases like obesity, inflammatory bowel disease, and multiple sclerosis. These bacteria produce vitamin B12 and appear to help other beneficial gut microbes thrive. Scientists haven't been able to grow them in a lab yet, but the discovery could eventually reshape how we measure gut health.
articles.takeaway: You can't supplement CAG-170 yet. But the research points back to the same basics: a varied, fiber-rich diet is the best way to support a diverse microbiome — including the bacteria we haven't even fully discovered.
articles.pexelsQuery: fresh vegetables colorful plate healthy food

articles.headline: Not Drinking Enough Water Is Making Your Stress Response Worse
articles.body: A study from Liverpool John Moores University found that under-hydrated people had cortisol levels more than 50% higher during a standardized stress test compared to well-hydrated participants. Both groups felt equally anxious — but only the low-fluid group showed a significant biological stress spike. Researchers say people in the low-fluid group didn't even feel thirstier, but their urine was darker and more concentrated. The finding suggests poor hydration amplifies the body's stress response even when you don't feel dehydrated.
articles.takeaway: Before a big presentation, a hard workout, or a stressful day with the kids — drink your water first. It won't eliminate stress, but it may stop your body from overreacting to it.

product.name: Skratch Labs Hydration Mix
product.blurb: Clean hydration without artificial junk. What Chris uses on long training days.

articles.topic: sleep schedule irregularity disease risk 172 conditions
articles.headline: It's Not Just How Long You Sleep. It's How Consistently You Sleep.
articles.body: A large international study published in Health Data Science analyzed objective sleep data from over 88,000 adults and found that irregular sleep habits — like inconsistent bedtimes — were linked to significantly higher risk across 172 diseases. Going to bed after 12:30am was associated with a 2.5-fold higher risk of liver disease. Poor circadian rhythm stability increased gangrene risk by over 2.6 times. The study also found that objective data challenged the idea that sleeping more than 9 hours is harmful — a claim that previous self-reported research had inflated.
articles.takeaway: Consistency matters as much as quantity. Locking in a regular bedtime — even on weekends — may do more for your long-term health than adding an extra hour of sleep here and there.

product.name: LectroFan White Noise Machine
product.blurb: Blocks ambient noise so your sleep is actually restorative.

quote.text: The mind is the athlete; the body is simply the means it uses.
quote.author: Kareem Abdul-Jabbar
quote.context: NBA Hall of Famer, 6x champion, and one of the most mentally disciplined athletes in sports history.

workout.title: Dead Hang and Row Strength Builder
equipment: sturdy table or low bar for rows
workout.warmup: Spend 2 minutes doing arm circles, shoulder rolls, and cat-cow stretches to open up the thoracic spine. Finish with 30 seconds of passive dead hang at low intensity to decompress the spine and prime your grip.

exercises.name: Dead Hang
exercises.notes: Hang from a pull-up bar with a full grip, shoulders active (not shrugged up to ears). Builds grip strength and shoulder health. Reduce time if grip fails before shoulders.

exercises.name: Inverted Table Row
exercises.notes: Use a sturdy table or low bar. Body straight, heels on floor, pull chest to the bar. Elevate feet on a chair to increase difficulty. Squeeze shoulder blades at the top.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Apart
exercises.notes: Hold a resistance band at shoulder height with both hands. Pull apart until arms are fully extended to sides. Slow and controlled — feel the rear delts and upper back work.

exercises.name: Scapular Pull-Up
exercises.notes: Hang from the bar, then depress and retract your shoulder blades without bending the elbows — a small but powerful movement. Great for shoulder health and lat activation.

exercises.name: Hollow Body Hold
exercises.notes: Lie on your back, arms overhead, lower back pressed into floor. Raise legs and shoulders slightly. This is the core position that makes every pulling movement stronger.

workout.cooldown: Hang passively for 30 seconds, then move into a chest-open doorway stretch and a supine spinal twist on each side. Focus on slow nasal breathing throughout.

product.name: Gymnastic Rings - Double Circle Wood
product.blurb: Chris trains on rings 2x per week. The most effective upper body tool that fits in a bag.

meal.title: Seared Salmon and White Bean Skillet
ingredients: 1 can (400g) white cannellini beans, drained and rinsed
meal.instructions: 1. Pat salmon dry, season with smoked paprika, salt, and pepper. Heat olive oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and sear salmon skin-side down for 4 minutes, then flip and cook 2–3 more minutes. Remove and rest. 2. In the same pan over medium heat, add garlic and cook 60 seconds until fragrant. Add white beans and stir to coat. 3. Add baby spinach and stir until wilted, about 2 minutes. Squeeze in lemon juice. 4. Plate the beans and spinach, top with salmon fillets, and garnish with parsley.
meal.tip: Don't overcrowd the pan when searing the salmon — you want crispy skin, not steaming. A hot, dry pan is the key.

product.name: Greater Goods Digital Food Scale
product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
